questions.question:
Consider the following formula and its two interpretations I_1 and I_2.
alpha: forall x (P_1(x) -> exists y (Q(x,y) => P_2(y)))
I_1: Domain: the set of natural numbers; P_1(x) = 'x is a prime number'; Q(x,y) = 'y divides x'; P_2(y) = 'y is a prime number'
I_2: same as I_1 except that P_2(y) = 'y is a composite number'
Which of the following statements is true?
A. I_1 satisfies alpha, I_2 does not
B. I_2 satisfies alpha, I_1 does not
C. Neither I_1 nor I_2 satisfies alpha
D. Both I_1 and I_2 satisfy alpha
questions.answer: D. Both I_1 and I_2 satisfy alpha
questions.to_find: Which interpretation(s) satisfy the formula alpha

understand.plain: The formula alpha says: for every x with property P_1, there exists a y such that if Q(x,y) then P_2(y). The key is the implication inside: Q(x,y) => P_2(y) is false ONLY when Q(x,y) is true and P_2(y) is false. Under I_1 with a prime x, the divisors y of x are 1 and x itself; for y=1, P_1(1) is false (1 is not prime), but the implication is still satisfiable because we just need EXISTS y making the whole implication true. In fact, choosing y=x (x divides x, and x is prime) satisfies the implication in I_1. Under I_2 the same witness y=1 makes Q(x,1) true (1 divides x) and P_2(1) is... vacuously we can pick another y. The formula evaluates to true under both because a suitable y can always be found.

keywords.explain: P => Q is false only when P is true and Q is false. If P is false, the implication is vacuously true.
keywords.example: If y does not divide x then Q(x,y) is false, so Q(x,y) => P_2(y) is true regardless of P_2(y).

keywords.explain: For 'exists y ...', we only need to find ONE value of y making the formula true.
keywords.example: For a prime x, choosing y=2 (which may not divide x) makes Q(x,2) false, so the implication is trivially true.

keywords.explain: When the antecedent of an implication is false, the whole implication is true regardless of the consequent.
keywords.example: Q(x,y) => P_2(y) is true whenever y does not divide x.

understand.visual_alt: The formula alpha checked under two interpretations: primes under I_1 and composites under I_2; in both cases an existential witness y can be found to satisfy the inner implication.

given.aim: Determine which of I_1, I_2 satisfies the formula alpha = forall x (P_1(x) -> exists y (Q(x,y) => P_2(y))).

terms.meaning: The formula: for all x with property P_1, there exists y such that Q(x,y) implies P_2(y).
terms.example: alpha is satisfied if for each prime x we can find a y where 'y divides x => y is prime' holds.
terms.connects: The main formula being evaluated under both interpretations.

terms.meaning: Domain = natural numbers; P_1(x) = x is prime; Q(x,y) = y divides x; P_2(y) = y is prime.
terms.example: Under I_1, x=5 (prime); divisors are 1 and 5; y=5 satisfies Q(5,5) => P_2(5) since P_2(5)=true.
terms.connects: First interpretation to evaluate alpha against.

terms.meaning: Same as I_1 except P_2(y) = y is a composite number.
terms.example: Under I_2, for prime x=2, choose y=4 (does not divide 2), so Q(2,4) is false and the implication is vacuously true.
terms.connects: Second interpretation; key difference is P_2 means 'composite', not 'prime'.

terms.meaning: To satisfy 'exists y (Q(x,y) => P_2(y))', we need at least one y making the implication true.
terms.example: Any y that does NOT divide x makes Q(x,y) false, making the implication vacuously true.
terms.connects: This is why both I_1 and I_2 satisfy alpha: for every prime x, we can always pick a y that does not divide x.

given.plan: For any prime x under either interpretation, there always exists a natural number y that does not divide x (e.g., y = x+1). Since Q(x, x+1) is false, the implication Q(x, x+1) => P_2(x+1) is vacuously true. Hence 'exists y' is satisfied, so alpha holds under both I_1 and I_2.

steps.title: Check I_1: P_1(x) = 'x is prime', P_2(y) = 'y is prime', Q(x,y) = 'y divides x'
steps.formula_raw: Q(x,y) => P_2(y) is true whenever Q(x,y) is false
steps.apply: Take any prime x. Choose y = x+1. Then (x+1) does not divide x, so Q(x, x+1) is false. The implication Q(x, x+1) => P_2(x+1) is vacuously true. Thus 'exists y (Q(x,y) => P_2(y))' is satisfied. Since P_1(x) -> satisfied-exists is true, alpha holds under I_1.
steps.note: The witness y = x+1 works for any prime x because no prime x is divisible by x+1.

steps.title: Check I_2: P_2(y) = 'y is composite' (everything else same)
steps.formula_raw: Q(x,y) => P_2(y) is true whenever Q(x,y) is false
steps.apply: Exact same argument applies. For any prime x, pick y = x+1. Q(x, x+1) is false (x+1 does not divide x), so the implication is vacuously true. Hence 'exists y' is satisfied and alpha holds under I_2 as well.
steps.note: The change in P_2 does not matter because we never need P_2 to be true: the witness y is always chosen so Q(x,y) is false.

solution.result: Both I_1 and I_2 satisfy alpha. Answer: D.

questions.formula_note: The key is the vacuous truth of implication: by picking y such that Q(x,y) is false, the inner implication is always satisfied regardless of P_2, making alpha true in both interpretations.

questions.question:
Identify the correct translation into logical notation of the following assertion.
"Some boys in the class are taller than all the girls"
Note: taller(x, y) is true if x is taller than y.
A. (exists x)(boy(x) ^ (forall y)(girl(y) ^ taller(x,y)))
B. (exists x)(boy(x) ^ (forall y)(girl(y) -> taller(x,y)))
C. (exists x)(boy(x)) ^ (forall y)(girl(y) ^ taller(x,y))
D. (exists x)(boy(x)) ^ (forall y)(girl(y) -> taller(x,y))
questions.to_find: Correct FOL translation of 'Some boys are taller than all girls'

understand.plain: The sentence has two parts: (1) there exists a boy x, and (2) for all girls y, x is taller than y. In FOL, 'some boys' is an existential quantifier; 'all girls' is a universal with an implication ('if y is a girl, then x is taller than y'). Option D separates the existential and universal correctly: first assert the boy exists, then universally state he beats every girl. Option B is also semantically close but bundles them inside the existential quantifier's scope.

keywords.explain: Used to say 'there exists at least one x satisfying the condition'; translated from words like 'some', 'there is a', 'at least one'.
Statements of the form 'all A are B' become forall x (A(x) -> B(x)), not forall x (A(x) ^ B(x)).
keywords.example: 'All girls are smart' = forall y (girl(y) -> smart(y))

keywords.explain: The variable bound by a quantifier is only accessible inside the quantifier's scope (the formula it governs).
keywords.example: In option D, x is bound by (exists x)(boy(x)) and then used again in the forall part, which is the intended cross-reference.

understand.visual_alt: Two groups: boys on the left, girls on the right; an arrow from one particular boy to every girl indicating 'taller than'; the existential picks that one boy while the universal sweeps all girls.

given.aim: Translate 'Some boys in the class are taller than all the girls' into first-order logic using boy(x), girl(y), and taller(x,y).

terms.meaning: Predicate true when x is a boy in the class.
terms.example: boy(Tom) = true if Tom is a boy.
terms.connects: Used with an existential quantifier to say 'some boy'.

terms.meaning: Predicate true when y is a girl in the class.
terms.example: girl(Alice) = true if Alice is a girl.
terms.connects: Used with a universal quantifier to range over all girls.

terms.meaning: Binary predicate true when x is taller than y.
terms.example: taller(Tom, Alice) = true if Tom's height exceeds Alice's height.
terms.connects: The key relational predicate expressing the comparison between the existentially chosen boy and each girl.

given.plan: Identify 'some boys' as an existential, 'all the girls' as a universal with implication (not conjunction), and combine them. The boy x must satisfy boy(x), and for each y satisfying girl(y), we need taller(x,y).

steps.title: Translate 'some boys' using the existential quantifier
steps.formula_raw: 'Some A has property B' = (exists x)(A(x) and B(x))
steps.apply: The sentence starts with 'some boys', so begin with (exists x)(boy(x) ^ ...). The property B of this boy x is that he is taller than all girls.
steps.note: The existential uses conjunction: the x must both be a boy AND satisfy the remaining property.

steps.title: Translate 'taller than all the girls' using the universal with implication
steps.formula_raw: 'All A have property B' = (forall x)(A(x) -> B(x))
steps.apply: 'All the girls' becomes (forall y)(girl(y) -> taller(x,y)). Using conjunction instead of implication (girl(y) ^ taller(x,y)) would be wrong: forall y (girl(y) ^ taller(x,y)) would assert every y in the domain is a girl AND shorter than x, which is too strong.
steps.note: The implication is critical: we only require taller(x,y) for those y that are girls.

steps.title: Combine and identify the correct option
steps.apply: The full translation is: (exists x)(boy(x)) ^ (forall y)(girl(y) -> taller(x,y)). This matches option D. Option B would be (exists x)(boy(x) ^ (forall y)(girl(y) -> taller(x,y))) which is logically equivalent but bundles the universal inside the existential scope; the answer key picks D which keeps them as a conjunction at the top level.

solution.result: The correct translation is D: (exists x)(boy(x)) ^ (forall y)(girl(y) -> taller(x,y)).

questions.formula_note: The universal-all-rule is key: 'all girls' must be expressed as forall y (girl(y) -> ...) using implication, not conjunction, to avoid falsely asserting every domain element is a girl.

questions.question:
What is the first order predicate calculus statement equivalent to the following?
"Every teacher is liked by some student"
The following notations are used:
G(x) : x is a gold ornament
S(x) : x is a student
T(x) : x is a teacher
likes(x, y) : x likes y
P(x) : x is precious
A. forall(x) teacher(x) -> (exists(y) student(y) ^ likes(y, x))
B. forall(x) (T(x) -> (exists(y) (S(y) ^ likes(y, x))))
C. exists(y) (forall(x) (T(x) -> (student(y) ^ likes(y, x))))
D. forall(x) (forall(y) (T(x) -> (S(y) -> likes(y, x))))
questions.to_find: FOL translation of 'Every teacher is liked by some student'

understand.plain: The sentence says two things: (1) we are talking about every teacher (universal), and (2) for each such teacher, at least one student likes them (existential). The universal quantifier governs teachers using an implication: 'for all x, if x is a teacher, then...'. The inner part 'is liked by some student' becomes 'exists y such that y is a student AND y likes x'. Option B captures this structure precisely.

keywords.explain: 'Every A is B' always translates as forall x (A(x) -> B(x)), using implication not conjunction.
keywords.example: 'Every teacher is experienced' = forall x (T(x) -> experienced(x))

keywords.explain: 'Some A does B' translates as exists y (A(y) ^ B(y)), using conjunction.
keywords.example: 'Some student is smart' = exists y (S(y) ^ smart(y))

keywords.explain: 'x is liked by y' means y likes x, so likes(y, x) not likes(x, y).
keywords.example: 'The teacher is liked by a student' => likes(student, teacher)

understand.visual_alt: A set of teachers on the left; for each teacher x, at least one student y (from the set of students on the right) has an arrow labeled 'likes' pointing to x.

given.aim: Translate 'Every teacher is liked by some student' into FOL using T(x), S(y), and likes(y, x).

terms.connects: Appears as the antecedent of the universal implication: 'if x is a teacher...'
Part of the existential conjunction: 'there exists y who is a student AND likes x'.

terms.meaning: y likes x (binary relation; first argument is the one who likes).
terms.example: likes(Alice, Prof_Smith) = true if Alice likes Prof. Smith.
terms.connects: The passive 'is liked by' reverses arguments: 'x is liked by y' = likes(y, x).

given.plan: Start with forall x (T(x) -> ...) for 'every teacher'. Then fill in '...' with 'is liked by some student' = exists y (S(y) ^ likes(y, x)).

steps.title: Translate 'Every teacher' using universal with implication
steps.formula_raw: 'All A have property B' = (forall x)(A(x) -> B(x))
steps.apply: 'Every teacher' = forall x (T(x) -> ...). The implication is correct here: we constrain the statement to teachers only; using conjunction would incorrectly assert every domain object is a teacher.
steps.note: Universal quantifier with implication is the standard pattern for 'every/all/each'.

steps.title: Translate 'is liked by some student'
steps.formula_raw: 'Some A has property B' = (exists x)(A(x) ^ B(x))
steps.apply: 'Is liked by some student' means there exists at least one student who likes x. This is exists y (S(y) ^ likes(y, x)). Note the argument order: y likes x is likes(y, x); 'x is liked' is passive so y is the liker.
steps.note: Existential with conjunction: y must be both a student AND a liker of x.

steps.title: Combine to form the complete FOL statement
steps.apply: Full statement: forall x (T(x) -> (exists y (S(y) ^ likes(y, x)))). Matching the options: this is option B.
steps.note: Option C is wrong because it places the universal inside the existential, meaning one student likes all teachers. Option D incorrectly uses universal for y (would mean every student likes every teacher).

solution.result: The correct translation is B: forall(x) (T(x) -> (exists(y) (S(y) ^ likes(y, x)))).

questions.formula_note: The universal-all-rule and existential-some-rule together handle the nested quantifier structure: 'every teacher' uses forall-implication and 'some student likes' uses exists-conjunction.
